# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a composite material. With a neat sketch, explain the role of matrix and reinforcement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A composite material is an engineered, heterogeneous material made by combining two or more distinct constituents — a **matrix** (continuous phase) and a **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — to obtain properties superior to those of the individual components.

**Matrix functions:** (1 mark)
- Binds and holds the reinforcement in place.
- Transfers applied load to the reinforcement through interfacial shear.
- Protects fibres from environmental damage (moisture, abrasion, chemicals).

**Reinforcement functions:** (1 mark)
- Carries the bulk of the mechanical load.
- Provides high strength, stiffness, and toughness.
- Restricts crack propagation, deflecting or branching cracks.

**Example:** Carbon fibre (reinforcement) + epoxy resin (matrix) used in aircraft wings. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intrinsic | Extrinsic |
|---|---|---|
| Purity | Pure, undoped | Doped with impurities |
| Carrier concentration | $n = p = n_i$ | $n \neq p$ (one dominates) |
| Conductivity control | Temperature only | Temperature + doping level |
| Examples | Pure Si, pure Ge | Si doped with P (n-type), Si doped with B (p-type) |

For n-type: Group V dopant (P, As) provides extra electron as majority carrier. (1 mark)
For p-type: Group III dopant (B, Ga) creates hole as majority carrier. (1 mark)
The product $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ holds for both. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Composite + Smart Material
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite contains **40% volume fraction of fibres**. Given:
- $E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm$^3$, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm$^3$
- $\sigma_f = 3.5$ GPa (fibre tensile strength), $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa

Compute:
(i) The composite density. (2 marks)
(ii) The longitudinal Young's modulus using the Voigt model. (2 marks)
(iii) The longitudinal tensile strength using the Rule of Mixtures. (3 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **shape memory effect** in Nitinol. List two engineering applications. (4 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a)(i) Composite density** (2 marks)

Using the Rule of Mixtures:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

Given $V_f = 0.40$, so $V_m = 0.60$.

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60) = 1.020 + 0.720 = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**(a)(ii) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt)** (2 marks)

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

$$E_c = (70)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60) = 28.0 + 2.1 = 30.1 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 30.1 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(a)(iii) Longitudinal tensile strength** (3 marks)

Using the Rule of Mixtures for strength (assuming fibre-dominated fracture):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

$$\sigma_c = (3500)(0.40) + (80)(0.60) = 1400 + 48 = 1448 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 1.45 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Note:** For exact calculation, we use $\sigma_m$ at the failure strain of the fibre ($\varepsilon_f = \sigma_f / E_f = 3500/70000 = 0.05$). At that strain, $\sigma_m' = E_m \cdot \varepsilon_f = 3.5 \times 10^3 \times 0.05 = 175$ MPa (above its actual $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa, so the matrix has already failed). The corrected strength is:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' V_m = 1400 + 175 \times 0.6 = 1505 \text{ MPa}$$

**(b) Shape Memory Effect in Nitinol** (4 marks)

Nitinol (Ni-Ti alloy) exhibits the shape memory effect due to a reversible **solid-state phase transformation** between:
- **Martensite** (stable at low temperature, monoclinic, soft, easily deformed)
- **Austenite** (stable at high temperature, cubic, stiff, "remembers" original shape)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Step 1 High T Austenite] --> nodeY[Step 2 Cool to Martensite]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Step 3 Deform at Low T]
    nodeZ --> nodeW[Step 4 Heat above Af]
    nodeW --> nodeR[Original Shape Recovered]
```

**Steps:**
1. Form the desired shape in austenite phase (high T). (0.5 mark)
2. Cool below $M_f$ to martensite. (0.5 mark)
3. Deform plastically at low T — this is "pseudo-plastic" deformation. (0.5 mark)
4. Heat above $A_f$ → transformation back to austenite → original shape recovered. (0.5 mark)

**Applications:** (2 marks, 1 each)
- **Biomedical stents** — collapsed for insertion, expands to original shape on warming to body temperature.
- **Couplings and actuators** in aerospace (F-14 Tomcat hydraulic line couplings).
- Eyeglass frames, robotic grippers, thermal actuators in micro-fluidics.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Semiconductor + Nano
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** For an intrinsic semiconductor at 300 K, the effective densities of states are $N_c = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $N_v = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, and the band gap is $E_g = 1.12$ eV.
- (i) Calculate the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$. (3 marks)
- (ii) If the semiconductor is doped with $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$ donors, find the electron and hole concentrations. (2 marks)
- (iii) Calculate the conductivity given $\mu_n = 1350$ cm$^2$/V·s and $\mu_p = 480$ cm$^2$/V·s. (2 marks)

**(b)** Explain **quantum confinement** in nanomaterials. Derive the qualitative dependence of band gap on particle radius using the particle-in-a-box model. (4 marks)

**Use** $k_B T = 0.0259$ eV at 300 K.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a)(i) Intrinsic carrier concentration** (3 marks)

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{N_c N_v} = \sqrt{(2.8 \times 10^{19})(1.04 \times 10^{19})} = \sqrt{2.912 \times 10^{38}} \approx 1.706 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

Exponent:

$$\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T} = \frac{1.12}{2 \times 0.0259} = \frac{1.12}{0.0518} \approx 21.62$$

$$\exp(-21.62) \approx 4.04 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$n_i = (1.706 \times 10^{19})(4.04 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$\boxed{n_i \approx 6.89 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

**(a)(ii) Extrinsic carrier concentrations** (2 marks)

For an n-type semiconductor, $n \approx N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$ (complete ionisation).

Using the mass-action law:

$$p = \frac{n_i^2}{n} = \frac{(6.89 \times 10^9)^2}{10^{16}} = \frac{4.75 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 4.75 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

**(a)(iii) Conductivity** (2 marks)

$$\sigma = q(n \mu_n + p \mu_p)$$

Since $n \gg p$, the hole term is negligible:

$$\sigma \approx q \cdot n \cdot \mu_n = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{16})(1350)$$

$$\sigma = 2.16 \text{ S/cm} = 216 \text{ S/m}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 216 \text{ S/m}}$$

**(b) Quantum Confinement in Nanomaterials** (4 marks)

When a semiconductor particle's size becomes comparable to (or smaller than) the **de Broglie wavelength of charge carriers** (typically a few nm), the charge carriers are spatially confined. This leads to **discretisation of energy levels** (similar to a particle-in-a-box).

**Derivation — Particle in a 3-D box of side $L$:**

For an electron confined in a sphere of radius $r$, the zero-point energy is:

$$E = \frac{h^2}{8 m_e r^2}$$

As $r$ decreases, $E$ **increases as $1/r^2$**, pushing the conduction band upward.

**Total band gap:**

$$E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \frac{h^2}{8 m_e r^2} - \text{(Coulomb correction)}$$

**Implications:** (2 marks)
- Smaller particles → larger band gap → bluer emission.
- **CdSe quantum dots** tune from red (large) to blue (small).
- Enables **band-gap engineering** without changing chemistry.
- Basis of **QLED displays**, **bio-imaging tags**, and **solar cell absorbers**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the closure relation $V_f + V_m = 1$** — if the question gives $V_f$, the examiner expects $V_m = 1 - V_f$ explicitly written, not assumed.
> 2. **Voigt vs Reuss confusion** — Voigt is for **load along fibres (iso-strain)**, Reuss is for **load transverse to fibres (iso-stress)**. Using the wrong one is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Exponent in $n_i$ formula** — students often write $E_g / (k_B T)$ instead of $E_g / (2 k_B T)$. The factor of 2 comes from $n = p = n_i$; both factors of $\sqrt{}$ contribute.
> 4. **Units trap** — in the conductivity formula, mixing cm$^{-3}$ and m$^{-3}$, or cm$^2$/V·s and m$^2$/V·s, leads to a numerical error of $10^6$.
> 5. **Biomaterials misconception** — confusing "biocompatible" with "bioinert". Bioglass is biocompatible but **bioactive**.
> 6. **Shape Memory** — students forget to mention that the deformation must be in the **martensitic state** for the effect to work.
> 7. **Nano size** — anything $\leq 100$ nm in at least one dimension counts as "nano". 1 µm is **not** nano.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

**Composite Materials:**
- ✔ Composite = **Matrix (continuous)** + **Reinforcement (dispersed)** + **Interface**.
- ✔ **Rule of Mixtures**: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ and $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Voigt).
- ✔ **Reuss model**: $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (transverse loading).
- ✔ Always remember: $V_f + V_m = 1$.
- ✔ **Types**: PMC (polymer), MMC (metal), CMC (ceramic); FRC, particle-reinforced, structural.

**Semiconductors:**
- ✔ Conductivity $10^{-5}$ to $10^3$ S/m; **Si = 1.12 eV**, **Ge = 0.67 eV**, **GaAs = 1.42 eV**.
- ✔ $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp(-E_g / 2k_B T)$; $k_B T = 0.0259$ eV at 300 K.
- ✔ **Mass-action law**: $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ — holds in **any** semiconductor at equilibrium.
- ✔ $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ — design levers: **doping (sets $n$)** and **mobility (sets $\mu$)**.
- ✔ **N-type** = Group V donor; **P-type** = Group III acceptor.

**Biomaterials:**
- ✔ Three regimes: **Bioinert** (Ti, Al$_2$O$_3$), **Bioactive** (Hydroxyapatite, Bioglass), **Bioresorbable** (PLA, PGA, Mg).
- ✔ **Biocompatibility** = performing function without adverse host response.
- ✔ **Applications**: orthopaedic implants, dental, cardiovascular stents, scaffolds, drug delivery.

**Smart Materials:**
- ✔ **Piezoelectric** (PZT, BaTiO$_3$): $D = d\sigma + \varepsilon E$.
- ✔ **SMA (Nitinol)**: Martensite (deform) → Austenite (recover) on heating above $A_f$.
- ✔ **Magnetostrictive** (Terfenol-D): $\Delta \ell/\ell = \lambda_s$ under magnetic field.
- ✔ **Electrochromic**: smart windows, displays.
- ✔ **MR / ER fluids**: viscosity changes on field application.

**Nanoengineered Materials:**
- ✔ Size regime: **1–100 nm** in at least one dimension.
- ✔ **SA:V = 3/r (sphere), 6/L (cube)** — explodes as size shrinks.
- ✔ **Quantum confinement**: $E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + h^2/(8 m_e r^2)$.
- ✔ **Families**: Carbon (CNT, graphene, C$_{60}$), Metal oxide, Dendrimers, Nanocomposites.
- ✔ Applications: **sunscreens** (TiO$_2$ ZnO), **structural composites** (CNT-epoxy), **displays** (QLED), **drug delivery** (dendrimers).

**Cross-Cutting Mnemonic — "C-S-B-S-N":**
**C**omposite → **S**emiconductor → **B**iomaterial → **S**mart → **N**ano. The five material classes in the KTU Module 1 syllabus, in the order of the official curriculum.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — OEBBT722 Advanced Materials**
