## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Material Class Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Engineering Material Selection]
    M1{Material Class?}
    M2[Metals: Metallic Bonding, Dislocations Mobile]
    M3[Ceramics: Ionic and Covalent Bonding, Dislocations Immobile]
    M4[Polymers: Covalent Chains plus Secondary Bonds]

    M2 --> M2A[Elastic: Bond Stretch E equals 70 to 400 GPa]
    M2 --> M2B[Plastic: Dislocation Glide on Slip Planes]
    M2 --> M2C[Failure: Ductile with Cup and Cone Fracture]

    M3 --> M3A[Elastic: Stiff Bonds E equals 100 to 1000 GPa]
    M3 --> M3B[Plastic: Negligible at Room Temperature]
    M3 --> M3C[Failure: Brittle Cleavage Fracture]

    M4 --> M4A[Elastic: Chain Uncoiling E equals 0.1 to 10 GPa]
    M4 --> M4B[Plastic: Chain Sliding and Orientation]
    M4 --> M4C[Failure: Crazing plus Shear Yielding]

    M0 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M1 --> M3
    M1 --> M4
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Elastic Constant Interrelationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Young Modulus E]
    C2[Shear Modulus G]
    C3[Bulk Modulus K]
    C4[Poisson Ratio nu]

    C1 -- E equals 2G times 1 plus nu --- C2
    C2 -- G equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu divided by 2 times 1 plus nu --- C3
    C3 -- K equals E divided by 3 times 1 minus 2nu --- C4
    C4 -- nu equals E divided by 2G minus 1 --- C1

    C1 -- E equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu --- C3
    C2 -- G equals E divided by 2 times 1 plus nu --- C4
```

```mermaid
    D2 --> D2A[Dislocation Slip on Close Packed Planes]
    D2 --> D2B[Twinning in HCP and BCC Metals]
    D2 --> D2C[Grain Boundary Sliding at High Temperature]

    D3 --> D3A[Chain Uncoiling and Rotation]
    D3 --> D3B[Chain Sliding past Each Other]
    D3 --> D3C[Crazing with Microvoid Formation]

    D4 --> D4A[Limited Dislocation Motion]
    D4 --> D4B[Diffusional Flow at High Temperature]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define elastic and plastic deformation. Give two key differences between them."**

**Model Answer:**

**Elastic Deformation:** The reversible, time-independent change in shape of a material under an applied load, which is fully recovered upon unloading. It occurs by **stretching of interatomic bonds** without permanent atomic displacement. It is governed by **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) and the strain is typically small (< 0.5% for metals).

**Plastic Deformation:** The permanent, irreversible change in shape of a material under an applied load that exceeds the **yield strength (σy)**. It is **not recovered** upon unloading. In crystalline solids, it occurs by **dislocation motion and slip** along close-packed crystallographic planes; in polymers, it occurs by **chain sliding and disentanglement**. Strain can be very large (10–40% for ductile metals).

| Two Key Differences | Elastic Deformation | Plastic Deformation |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Permanent / irreversible |
| Atomic mechanism | Bond stretching only | Bond breaking + atomic plane slip |
| Magnitude | Small strain (≈ 0.1–0.5%) | Large strain (1–40%+) |
| Energy storage | Stored as strain energy | Dissipated mostly as heat |
| Governing law | Hooke's law (linear) | Hollomon equation / plasticity theory |
| Governed by | Young's modulus $E$ | Yield strength $\sigma_y$, strain hardening $n$ |

*(3 marks awarded: 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for the comparison table key points)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"Explain the significance of the 0.2% offset yield strength. How is it determined from a stress–strain diagram?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** Many engineering materials, such as aluminum alloys, brass, and polymers, do **not exhibit a sharp yield point** in their stress–strain curves. For these materials, the transition from elastic to plastic behavior is gradual, and there is no clearly defined yield point to identify σy. To enable uniform design practice, engineers use the **0.2% offset yield strength (σ₀.₂)** as a **conventional, standardized** measure of the onset of plastic deformation. It represents the stress that produces a **permanent (plastic) strain of 0.2%** upon unloading.

**Determination Procedure:**
1. From the origin O of the engineering stress–strain curve, draw a line **parallel to the initial linear elastic portion**, but offset along the strain axis by 0.002 (i.e., 0.2%).
2. This offset line intersects the stress–strain curve at a point.
3. The **stress corresponding to this intersection point** is defined as the **0.2% offset yield strength, σ₀.₂**.

**Practical Use:** This is the standard yield strength quoted in material property tables (e.g., for AISI 6061-T6 aluminum, σ₀.₂ = 276 MPa) and is used directly in **design equations** (e.g., allowable load = σ₀.₂ × Area / Safety Factor).

*(3 marks: 1 for explaining need, 1 for the construction procedure, 1 for practical use)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't** write only definitions — KTU examiners award marks for **mechanisms, diagrams, numerical working, and tabulated comparisons**.
> - **Don't** confuse elastic and plastic constants — $E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$ are **elastic**; $\sigma_y$, $K_{strength}$, $n$ are **plastic**.
> - **Don't** forget to state the **units** explicitly. A correct numerical answer *without units* loses 1 mark.
> - **Always** draw a neat, labeled stress–strain diagram where applicable — it carries **2 marks** by itself.
> - In derivation problems, **show every algebraic step**. Skipping steps = losing partial marks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus ($E$), Shear Modulus ($G$), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) for an isotropic homogeneous linear elastic material. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass specimen has $E = 105$ GPa and $\nu = 0.34$. If a tensile stress of 180 MPa is applied, calculate the shear modulus, bulk modulus, axial strain, lateral strain, and the volumetric strain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The material is **homogeneous** (same properties at all points).
2. The material is **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
3. The material behaves **linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
4. Deformations are **small** (engineering strain approximation valid).

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 for full steps)
- Consider a unit cube under pure shear stress $\tau$.
- Equivalent principal stresses: $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ at +45°, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ at –45°.
- Using generalized Hooke's law: $\varepsilon_1 = \tau(1+\nu)/E$ and $\varepsilon_2 = -\tau(1+\nu)/E$.
- Shear strain: $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = 2\tau(1+\nu)/E$.
- Equating with $\gamma = \tau/G$: $\tau/G = 2\tau(1+\nu)/E$.
- Simplifying:
$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $E = 105 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\nu = 0.34$, $\sigma = 180$ MPa

**Step 1: Shear Modulus (G)**
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{105 \times 10^3}{2(1.34)} = \frac{105000}{2.68} = 39179.1 \text{ MPa} \approx 39.18 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2: Bulk Modulus (K)**
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{105 \times 10^3}{3(1 - 0.68)} = \frac{105000}{3 \times 0.32} = \frac{105000}{0.96} = 109375 \text{ MPa} \approx 109.38 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3: Axial Strain**
$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{180}{105 \times 10^3} = 1.714 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Lateral Strain**
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -0.34 \times 1.714 \times 10^{-3} = -5.829 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5: Volumetric Strain**
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_{axial}(1 - 2\nu) = 1.714 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.68) = 1.714 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.32$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 5.486 \times 10^{-4}$$

| Parameter | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $G$ | 39.18 | GPa |
| $K$ | 109.38 | GPa |
| $\varepsilon_{axial}$ | $1.714 \times 10^{-3}$ | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon_{lateral}$ | $-5.83 \times 10^{-4}$ | dimensionless |
| $\Delta V/V$ | $5.49 \times 10^{-4}$ | dimensionless |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Compare the elastic and plastic deformation behavior of metals, ceramics, and polymers with respect to: (i) atomic bonding, (ii) Young's modulus range, (iii) elastic strain limit, (iv) plastic deformation mechanism, and (v) typical fracture mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical specimen of initial diameter 12.5 mm and gauge length 50 mm is pulled in tension. The maximum load sustained is 65,000 N, at which point the gauge length is 58 mm and the diameter is 10.8 mm. Determine: (i) engineering stress at maximum load, (ii) engineering strain at maximum load, (iii) true stress at maximum load, and (iv) true strain at maximum load. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Property | Metals | Ceramics | Polymers |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Atomic bonding | Metallic bond (delocalized electron sea) | Ionic / covalent (strong, directional) | Covalent backbone + weak secondary bonds (van der Waals, H-bonds) |
| (ii) Young's Modulus (E) | 70–400 GPa | 100–1000 GPa (very stiff) | 0.1–10 GPa (compliant) |
| (iii) Elastic strain limit | ~0.1–0.5% | ~0.05–0.2% | ~0.5–5% (often non-linear) |
| (iv) Plastic mechanism | Dislocation glide on {111}/{110}/{0001} planes | Almost none at RT (dislocations immobile) — fracture precedes plasticity | Chain sliding, disentanglement, viscous flow |
| (v) Fracture mode | Ductile (cup-and-cone, dimpled) | Brittle (cleavage, transgranular) | Mixed (crazing + shear yielding) |

**Key Inferences (additional marks):**
- Ceramics are **stiffest but most brittle** — best under compression.
- Metals offer best balance of strength + toughness — most widely used.
- Polymers are **tough but compliant** — ideal for impact absorption.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 12.5$ mm, $l_0 = 50$ mm, $F_{max} = 65{,}000$ N
- $l_f = 58$ mm, $d_f = 10.8$ mm (at maximum load)

**Step 1: Original Cross-Sectional Area**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (12.5)^2}{4} = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Instantaneous Cross-Sectional Area at Maximum Load**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (10.8)^2}{4} = 91.61 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Engineering Stress at Maximum Load**
$$\sigma = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{65000 \text{ N}}{122.72 \text{ mm}^2} = 529.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Strain at Maximum Load**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{l_f - l_0}{l_0} = \frac{58 - 50}{50} = 0.16 \text{ (or 16\%)}$$

**Step 5: True Stress at Maximum Load**
$$\sigma_t = \frac{F_{max}}{A_f} = \frac{65000}{91.61} = 709.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Verify Using Conversion Formula**
$$\sigma_t = \sigma(1 + \varepsilon) = 529.6 \times (1 + 0.16) = 529.6 \times 1.16 = 614.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Note:** There is a small discrepancy (709.5 vs 614.3) because **volume is not perfectly conserved** during plastic deformation (voids/cavities form). KTU accepts either method, but the direct calculation $\sigma_t = F/A_f$ is **more accurate**.

**Step 7: True Strain at Maximum Load**
$$\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon) = \ln(1.16) = 0.1484 \text{ (or 14.84\%)}$$

**Step 8: Verify Using Direct Calculation**
$$\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\frac{l_f}{l_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{58}{50}\right) = \ln(1.16) = 0.1484 \checkmark$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma$ | 529.6 | MPa |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon$ | 0.16 | dimensionless |
| True Stress | $\sigma_t$ | 709.5 | MPa |
| True Strain | $\varepsilon_t$ | 0.1484 | dimensionless |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Elastic deformation** = bond stretching only, fully reversible, governed by Hooke's Law
- ☐ **Plastic deformation** = permanent, governed by dislocation motion (metals) or chain sliding (polymers)
- ☐ **Yield point (σy)** = boundary between elastic and plastic regions
- ☐ **0.2% offset method** = standard for materials without sharp yield point
- ☐ **Engineering stress** uses original area; **True stress** uses instantaneous area

### Critical Equations (must memorize)
- ☐ $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — Hooke's Law (uniaxial tension)
- ☐ $\tau = G\gamma$ — Hooke's Law (shear)
- ☐ $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$ — Poisson's Ratio
- ☐ $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ — Three elastic constants linked
- ☐ $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ and $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ — True stress/strain
- ☐ $\sigma_t = K\varepsilon_t^n$ — Hollomon equation (strain hardening)

### Comparative Material Facts
- ☐ **Stiffness order:** Ceramics > Metals > Polymers
- ☐ **Toughness order:** Metals > Polymers > Ceramics
- ☐ **Ceramic fracture = brittle** (no plastic region)
- ☐ **Polymer behavior = viscoelastic** (time and temperature dependent)
- ☐ **Metal plastic mechanism = dislocation glide** on close-packed planes

### Common Exam Pitfalls
- ☐ Forgetting to convert GPa ↔ MPa ($1$ GPa $= 10^3$ MPa)
- ☐ Confusing Tresca ($\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$) with Von Mises (more complex)
- ☐ Mixing up engineering strain (uses $l_0$) with true strain (uses instantaneous length)
- ☐ Forgetting the **negative sign** in Poisson's ratio convention
- ☐ Saying ceramics "have high strength" without specifying **compression** (they are weak in tension!)

### Numerical Skills
- ☐ Always **state units** in final answer
- ☐ Show **all intermediate steps** in derivations
- ☐ Verify answers by **substituting back** into original equations
- ☐ Use $\ln(x)$ for true strain, not $\log_{10}(x)$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. For **3-mark questions**: Definition + 1 example + 1 distinguishing feature = full marks
> 2. For **7-mark sub-parts**: Diagram + Explanation + Numerical example (if applicable)
> 3. For **derivations**: Assumptions → Starting equation → Step-by-step algebra → Final result → Physical interpretation
> 4. **Always** draw a labeled stress–strain diagram wherever the question mentions "explain elastic-plastic behavior" — this is a guaranteed 2 marks per KTU valuation key.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 Coverage Complete:** This comprehensive treatment of elastic and plastic behavior of metals, ceramics, and polymers provides a strong foundation for **Module 2 (Dislocations and Strengthening Mechanisms)** and **Module 3 (Fracture, Fatigue, and Creep)**. The concepts of dislocation motion introduced here will be expanded upon, and the stress–strain analysis will be linked to **failure analysis** in subsequent modules.

**Best of luck for your KTU examinations! Prepare well, draw neat diagrams, and state units explicitly — success is assured.** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Changes in the Microstructure of Materials due to Mechanical and Thermal Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT722 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Changes in the Microstructure of Materials due to Mechanical and Thermal Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Microstructure** refers to the internal architecture of a material at the **micrometer to nanometer scale**, encompassing features such as **grain size, grain boundaries, phase distribution, dislocation density, precipitates, and texture**. Under applied **mechanical load (stress/strain)** and **thermal load (temperature/time cycles)**, this microstructure undergoes dynamic reconfiguration, dictating the macroscopic mechanical, thermal, and functional properties of engineering materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The ability of a material to undergo microstructural modification (e.g., through cold working + annealing cycles) is the very foundation of **thermomechanical processing (TMP)** in advanced materials engineering. This forms the basis for designing high-strength steels, superalloys, and aerospace components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **"The Sponge Under a Squeeze and Bake Cycle"**
>
> Imagine a wet kitchen sponge (representing a polycrystalline metal with grains and water trapped at grain boundaries).
>
> - When you **squeeze** the sponge (Mechanical Load), water (analogous to **dislocations**) is pushed out, the sponge becomes denser, harder, but also more brittle. This is **Cold Working / Strain Hardening**.
> - When you **heat** the squeezed sponge in a microwave (Thermal Load), the sponge relaxes, the water redistributes, and new pores (grains) form. If heated too long, the pores grow large and the sponge weakens. This is **Annealing → Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain Growth**.
> - The combined **"Squeeze + Bake"** process is exactly what happens in a rolling mill followed by a furnace — the essence of thermomechanical processing.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** | Atomic scaling in diffusion |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Thermal energy per atom |
| Burgers Vector (Cu) | $\vec{b}$ | **0.2556 nm** | Magnitude of lattice distortion |
| Recrystallization Temp (Cu) | $T_R$ | **~0.4 T_m (K)** | Empirical thumb rule |
| Self-diffusion Activation Energy | $Q_d$ | **~200 kJ/mol (for Fe-α)** | Energy barrier for atomic motion |

### 1.4 Classification of Microstructural Changes

Microstructural alterations can be broadly classified as:

1. **Mechanically Induced Changes** (Cold Work, Hot Work, Fatigue, Creep)
2. **Thermally Induced Changes** (Recovery, Recrystallization, Grain Growth, Phase Transformation)
3. **Thermo-Mechanically Induced Changes** (Ausforming, Isoforming, Controlled Rolling)
4. **Radiation-Induced Changes** (Beyond KTU Module 1 scope)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Mechanical changes are largely **athermal** (defect-driven), while thermal changes are **diffusion-driven** (time and temperature dependent). The transition between these regimes is governed by the **Homo-Temperature (T_H)** threshold.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanically Induced Microstructural Changes

### 2.1.1 Plastic Deformation Mechanism

When a stress exceeding the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)** is applied, atomic planes slip past each other along the **slip system** (defined by **slip plane** and **slip direction**). The primary mechanisms include:

- **Dislocation Glide (Slip):** Conservative motion along close-packed planes.
- **Dislocation Climb:** Non-conservative motion requiring vacancy diffusion (dominant at high T).
- **Twinning:** Cooperative, mirror-image atomic displacement (dominant in HCP metals like Mg, Zn, Ti).

### 2.1.2 Cold Work (Strain Hardening) — Stepwise Logic

1. **Dislocation Multiplication:** Frank-Read sources generate new dislocation loops.
2. **Dislocation Entanglement:** Increased dislocation density ($\rho$) leads to mutual obstruction.
3. **Pile-up Formation:** Dislocations accumulate at grain boundaries, creating back-stresses.
4. **Cell Structure Development:** Dislocations self-organize into low-density cells separated by high-density walls.

> **The "Why":** The microstructure transforms from an *annealed, low-defect state* to a *highly deformed, saturated-defect state*, dramatically increasing strength but reducing ductility.

### 2.1.3 Strengthening Contribution (Taylor Equation)

The yield strength increment from cold work is given by the **Taylor Strengthening Relation**:

$$
\Delta \sigma_{CW} = \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{\rho}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = strengthening coefficient (**~0.3 to 0.5** for most metals)
- $G$ = Shear modulus (e.g., **80 GPa for steel, 26 GPa for Al**)
- $b$ = Magnitude of Burgers vector
- $\rho$ = Dislocation density (typically **10¹⁰ to 10¹⁶ m⁻²**)

## 2.2 Thermally Induced Microstructural Changes (The Three Stages of Annealing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Topic:** The three stages of annealing — **Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain Growth** — are extremely high-yield for board exams. Memorize the driving force and dominant mechanism for each.

### Stage 1: Recovery (Low-Temperature Annealing)

- **Temperature Range:** Below **0.3 T_m**
- **Driving Force:** Stored strain energy from cold work
- **Mechanism:** Vacancy annihilation, dislocation rearrangement (climb), polygonization
- **Microstructural Result:** Sharp decrease in point defect concentration; little change in grain shape or hardness
- **Property Effect:** Electrical conductivity recovers significantly; hardness nearly unchanged

### Stage 2: Recrystallization (Mid-Temperature Annealing)

- **Temperature Range:** **0.3 T_m to 0.5 T_m**
- **Driving Force:** Stored strain energy ($\Delta G_v$)
- **Mechanism:** Nucleation of strain-free new grains at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands), followed by growth
- **Microstructural Result:** New equiaxed, strain-free grains replace the deformed structure
- **Property Effect:** Sharp drop in hardness and strength; restoration of ductility

### Stage 3: Grain Growth (High-Temperature Annealing)

- **Temperature Range:** Above **0.5 T_m**
- **Driving Force:** Reduction in total grain boundary area (capillarity)
- **Mechanism:** Grain boundary migration; larger grains consume smaller ones
- **Microstructural Result:** Coarse-grained microstructure
- **Property Effect:** Further decrease in strength; possible embrittlement

## 2.3 Hot Working vs. Cold Working

| Parameter | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $< 0.3 \, T_m$ | $> 0.6 \, T_m$ |
| Recrystallization during deformation | No | Yes (dynamic) |
| Strain hardening retained | Yes | No |
| Final product surface | Smooth, accurate dimensions | Scaled, less accurate |
| Energy required | High | Lower (flow stress drops) |
| Typical products | Sheet, wire, foil | Forged shafts, rolled plates |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taylor Strengthening | $\Delta \sigma = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | Strength gain from dislocations | Pa |
| Hall-Petch Relation | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Grain boundary strengthening | Pa, m |
| Grain Growth Law | $D^n - D_0^n = K_0 t \exp(-Q_{gg}/RT)$ | Kinetics of grain coarsening | m, s |
| Arrhenius Diffusion | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q_d/RT)$ | Atomic mobility with temperature | m²/s |
| Recrystallization (Avrami) | $X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ | Fraction recrystallized | dimensionless |
| Critical Nucleation Radius | $r^* = 2 \gamma / \Delta G_v$ | Minimum stable nucleus size | m |
| Strain Energy Stored | $E_s = \alpha G b^2 \rho$ | Stored cold work energy | J/m³ |
| Steady-State Creep | $\dot{\epsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | High-T deformation rate | s⁻¹ |

> **Real-World Utility:** These equations govern the design of:
> - **Aerospace forging cycles** (Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718)
> - **Automotive sheet steel** (DP steels, TRIP steels)
> - **Power plant components** (creep-resistant steels for boilers)
> - **Heat treatment of tool steels** (martempering, austempering)

## 2.5 Hall-Petch Strengthening (Grain Boundary Mechanism)

Grain boundaries act as **dislocation barriers** because:
- Disorientation between adjacent grains requires slip direction change
- Atomic disorder at boundaries impedes dislocation motion

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

> **The "Why":** As grain size $d$ decreases, the number of grain boundaries per unit volume increases, requiring higher stress for dislocations to traverse. This is why **nanocrystalline materials** exhibit extraordinary strength.

### Limitation: Inverse Hall-Petch Effect

When grain size drops below a critical value (**~10-15 nm**), grain boundary sliding dominates, and the relationship inverts. This is a frontier research area in **nanomaterials engineering**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hall-Petch Relation from Dislocation Pile-Up Theory

**Goal:** Derive the relationship $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ from first principles.

### Step 1: Setting up the Pile-Up Geometry

Consider a grain of diameter $d$. A dislocation source (e.g., Frank-Read source) at the center emits $n$ dislocations under applied shear stress $\tau$. These dislocations pile up against the opposite grain boundary.

The number of dislocations in the pile-up is given by:

$$
n = \frac{k \cdot \tau \cdot d}{G \cdot b}
$$

**Logic:** The leading dislocation is held back by the boundary, while the trailing dislocations are pushed by the applied stress. The equilibrium count scales linearly with grain size.

### Step 2: Stress Concentration at the Pile-Up Tip

The stress concentration at the head of the pile-up acts on the next grain's dislocation source. From dislocation mechanics, the concentrated stress is:

$$
\tau_{tip} = n \cdot \tau
$$

Substituting $n$ from Step 1:

$$
\tau_{tip} = \frac{k \cdot \tau^2 \cdot d}{G \cdot b}
$$

**Logic:** A larger grain permits more dislocations in the pile-up, creating a sharper stress concentration at the boundary.

### Step 3: Activation of Slip in the Adjacent Grain

Yielding occurs when $\tau_{tip}$ reaches a critical value $\tau_c$ (the stress needed to activate slip in the next grain):

$$
\tau_c = \frac{k \cdot \tau^2 \cdot d}{G \cdot b}
$$

### Step 4: Solving for Applied Shear Stress

Rearranging the equation in Step 3 to solve for the applied stress $\tau$:

$$
\tau^2 = \frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{k \cdot d}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\tau = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{k}} \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

### Step 5: Conversion to Tensile Yield Strength

Converting shear stress to tensile yield stress using $\sigma_y = 2\tau$ (Tresca criterion approximation), and combining the constants into $\sigma_0$ (lattice friction stress) and $k_y$ (Hall-Petch coefficient):

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

> **Final Insight:** The $d^{-1/2}$ dependence is not empirical — it emerges rigorously from dislocation pile-up theory. This makes Hall-Petch one of the few **analytically derived** strength relations in materials science.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Microstructural Evolution Under Combined Loading

| Loading Condition | Dominant Mechanism | Microstructural Signature | Property Signature | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Rolling (Low T, High Strain)** | Dislocation multiplication, slip band formation | Elongated grains, dense dislocation tangles, cell walls | $\uparrow$ Hardness, $\uparrow$ Yield Strength, $\downarrow$ Ductility | Sheet metal for automotive panels |
| **Annealing @ 0.3 T_m (Recovery)** | Vacancy diffusion, dislocation climb | Polygonized subgrains, reduced point defects | $\uparrow$ Conductivity, ≈ constant hardness | Stress relief in welded structures |
| **Annealing @ 0.4 T_m (Recrystallization)** | Nucleation + growth of strain-free grains | New equiaxed grains replacing deformed structure | $\downarrow$ Hardness, $\uparrow$ Ductility | Softening for further forming |
| **Annealing @ 0.6 T_m (Grain Growth)** | Grain boundary migration | Coarse grains, fewer boundaries | $\downarrow$ Strength (Hall-Petch), possibly $\uparrow$ creep resistance | Limited — usually avoided |
| **Hot Forging (T > 0.6 T_m)** | Dynamic recrystallization | Refined, equiaxed dynamic grains | Moderate strength, good ductility | Turbine disks, crankshafts |
| **Cyclic Loading (Fatigue)** | Persistent slip bands (PSBs), extrusions/intrusions | Striations, subgrains, eventual crack initiation | Eventual failure at sub-yield stresses | Aircraft wings, bridge cables |
| **Constant Load @ High T (Creep)** | Dislocation climb, grain boundary sliding, diffusion flow | Cavities at GBs, subgrain formation | Time-dependent strain accumulation | Boiler tubes, gas turbine blades |
| **Quenching from Austenite** | Martensitic (diffusionless) shear transformation | Lath/plate martensite, high dislocation density | $\uparrow\uparrow$ Hardness, $\uparrow$ Brittleness | Hardened tool steel, rails |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A cold-worked copper specimen has a dislocation density of $10^{14}$ m⁻². If the original density was $10^{10}$ m⁻², calculate the increase in yield strength. Given: $\alpha = 0.4$, $G_{Cu} = 42$ GPa, $b = 0.2556$ nm.

### Step 1: Identify Formula

$$
\Delta \sigma = \alpha G b (\sqrt{\rho_2} - \sqrt{\rho_1})
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \sigma &= 0.4 \times (42 \times 10^9) \times (0.2556 \times 10^{-9}) \times (\sqrt{10^{14}} - \sqrt{10^{10}}) \\
&= 0.4 \times 42 \times 10^9 \times 0.2556 \times 10^{-9} \times (10^7 - 10^5) \\
&= 4.2936 \times (9.9 \times 10^6) \\
&\approx 42.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 42.5 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret Result

> A **42.5 MPa** strength increase confirms that cold work is a highly effective strengthening mechanism. In practice, copper yield strength rises from ~70 MPa (annealed) to ~330 MPa (heavily cold worked), consistent with this Taylor-type analysis.

## 3.4 Recrystallization Kinetics (Avrami Equation Derivation Outline)

The fraction of recrystallized volume $X$ at time $t$ follows:

$$
X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)
$$

Where:
- $k$ = temperature-dependent rate constant
- $n$ = Avrami exponent (**n = 3 to 4** for 3D nucleation and growth)

**Logic:** The unrecrystallized volume decays exponentially because nucleation occurs randomly and growth proceeds at constant velocity — analogous to radioactive decay. The exponent $n$ encodes the dimensionality of growth (1D, 2D, or 3D).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Recrystallization Temperature and State the Empirical Rule
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
Recrystallization temperature is defined as the **minimum temperature at which a cold-worked metal becomes completely recrystallized (≥ 95%) within approximately one hour of soaking time**.

**Empirical Rule:**

$$
T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m \text{ (in Kelvin)}
$$

Where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin.

**Example for Copper:** $T_m = 1356$ K, so $T_R \approx 0.4 \times 1356 \approx 542$ K $\approx 269$°C.

> **Examiner's Note (3-mark allocation):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for numerical illustration. Skipping the example forfeits 1 mark.
