## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (Option 1): Comprehensive Treatment of Thermal Load Effects

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1]**

**(a) Explain the three stages of annealing in a cold-worked metal with suitable microstructural diagrams. Discuss the driving force and dominant mechanism at each stage. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The three stages of annealing are sequentially activated as a cold-worked metal is progressively heated:

**Stage 1 — Recovery (Heating up to ~0.3 T_m):**
- **Driving Force:** Stored strain energy from cold work (primarily as point defect concentration and dislocation strain fields).
- **Dominant Mechanism:** 
  1. Annihilation of excess vacancies at grain boundaries and dislocations.
  2. Rearrangement of dislocations into lower-energy configurations (polygonization).
  3. Formation of **subgrain boundaries** — dislocation walls separating nearly perfect cells.
- **Microstructural Signature:** Subgrains visible under TEM; grain shape unchanged.
- **Property Change:** Electrical conductivity recovers; mechanical properties (hardness, strength) change only slightly.

**Stage 2 — Recrystallization (~0.3 to 0.5 T_m):**
- **Driving Force:** Stored strain energy density ($\Delta G_v$).
- **Dominant Mechanism:** 
  1. **Nucleation** of strain-free embryos at high-energy sites — pre-existing grain boundaries, deformation bands, twin boundaries, and second-phase particles.
  2. **Growth** of these nuclei by **strain-induced boundary migration (SIBM)** — the new grain boundary sweeps into the deformed matrix, leaving behind a defect-free region.
  3. Critical nucleus radius: $r^* = 2\gamma_{gb} / \Delta G_v$
- **Microstructural Signature:** New equiaxed, strain-free grains completely replace the deformed structure once recrystallization is complete.
- **Property Change:** Sharp drop in hardness and yield strength; restoration of ductility (e.g., cold-rolled copper drops from ~330 MPa to ~70 MPa).

**Stage 3 — Grain Growth (Above ~0.5 T_m):**
- **Driving Force:** Reduction in **total grain boundary area** (surface energy minimization).
- **Dominant Mechanism:**
  1. **Grain boundary migration** — atoms jump across the boundary from the concave (high-curvature, smaller grain) side to the convex (lower-curvature, larger grain) side.
  2. **Ostwald Ripening analogue:** Larger grains grow at the expense of smaller ones.
  3. Kinetics: $D^n - D_0^n = K_0 t \exp(-Q_{gg}/RT)$ (typically $n = 2$).
- **Microstructural Signature:** Progressively coarser grains; eventual bimodal or abnormal grain growth possible.
- **Property Change:** Further decrease in strength (Hall-Petch); possible loss of toughness.

> **Examiner's Insight:** Award full 7 marks only if the student explicitly identifies the **driving force**, **mechanism**, and **microstructural signature** for each stage. Omitting one of these typically costs 1-2 marks.

---

**(b) Derive the Hall-Petch equation from dislocation pile-up theory. Discuss the inverse Hall-Petch effect in nanocrystalline materials. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (as detailed in Section 3.1):**

The derivation proceeds in 5 steps:

1. **Pile-up formation:** $n$ dislocations pile up at a grain boundary of diameter $d$, with $n = k\tau d / Gb$.
2. **Stress concentration:** The tip stress is $\tau_{tip} = n\tau = k\tau^2 d / Gb$.
3. **Yield criterion:** Yielding in the adjacent grain begins when $\tau_{tip} = \tau_c$.
4. **Solving for applied stress:** $\tau = \sqrt{\tau_c G b / k} \cdot d^{-1/2}$.
5. **Converting to tensile yield:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$.

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = lattice friction stress (resistance of the single crystal)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (boundary strength parameter)

**Inverse Hall-Petch Effect:**

When grain size is reduced below a critical value $d_c \approx 10\text{–}15$ nm:

- The **volume fraction of grain boundary atoms** becomes comparable to the bulk.
- Deformation mechanism shifts from **dislocation-mediated slip** to **grain boundary sliding** and **Coble creep**.
- Strength **decreases** with further grain refinement, violating the classical Hall-Petch relation.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2} \quad \text{(Classical, } d > d_c \text{)}
$$

$$
\sigma_y \approx \sigma_0 + k_y' \cdot (\ln d)^{-1} \quad \text{(Inverse, } d < d_c \text{)}
$$

**Engineering Significance:** This defines the lower limit of grain refinement for structural strengthening, motivating research into alternative nanostructuring strategies such as **nanotwins, gradient nanostructures, and high-entropy alloys**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Option 2): Mechanical Load and Combined Effects

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1]**

**(a) With neat microstructural sketches, explain the changes in microstructure during cold working of a ductile metal. How does the dislocation density affect the yield strength? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Microstructural Evolution During Cold Working:**

| Stage | Description | Microstructural Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Initial Annealed State | Equiaxed grains, low dislocation density ($\rho_0 \approx 10^{10}$ m⁻²) | Sharp grain boundaries, clean interior |
| 2. Early Plastic Deformation (~2-5% strain) | Slip bands appear on surface; few dislocations | Parallel slip lines |
| 3. Moderate Deformation (10-20% strain) | Dislocation tangles, pile-ups at boundaries | Cell structure begins forming |
| 4. Heavy Deformation (>30% strain) | Elongated grains, dense dislocation walls, texture develops | Pancaked grain structure, preferred orientation |
| 5. Severe Deformation (>60% strain) | Saturation, sub-grain boundaries nearly amorphous | Highly refined, textured structure |

**Effect of Dislocation Density on Yield Strength (Taylor Equation):**

$$
\Delta \sigma = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}
$$

**Physical Explanation:**
- Each dislocation contributes a long-range stress field (~1/r) extending several Burgers vectors.
- As $\rho$ increases, the average inter-dislocation spacing decreases ($l \approx \rho^{-1/2}$).
- Dislocations must interact elastically and entangle, requiring higher applied stress to bow out and propagate.
- At saturation, $\rho$ can reach $10^{15}\text{–}10^{16}$ m⁻², producing a strength increase of **2-5× the annealed value**.

**Example:** Cold-drawn steel wire achieves tensile strengths of **2-3 GPa**, compared to ~0.5 GPa in the annealed state — entirely due to dislocation density enhancement.

---

**(b) Compare hot working and cold working processes. Explain dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization with their effects on the final microstructure. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (Marks allocated: 3):**

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $> 0.6 \, T_m$ | $< 0.3 \, T_m$ |
| Recrystallization | Concurrent with deformation | After deformation (static) |
| Strain hardening | Negligible (recovered dynamically) | Significant, retained |
| Final strength | Lower | Higher |
| Surface quality | Scaled, oxide layer | Smooth, bright |
| Dimensional accuracy | Poor | Excellent |
| Energy required | Lower (lower flow stress) | Higher |
| Equipment wear | Less | More |

**Dynamic Recovery (DRV) — Marks allocated: 2:**

- **Definition:** Continuous annihilation and rearrangement of dislocations during hot deformation.
- **Mechanism:** Cross-slip and climb of dislocations, facilitated by enhanced vacancy diffusion at high temperatures.
- **Microstructure:** Stable subgrain structure with low stored energy.
- **Limitation:** DRV is most prominent in **high stacking fault energy (SFE)** metals (Al, α-Fe, ferritic steels) where cross-slip is easy.

**Dynamic Recrystallization (DRX) — Marks allocated: 2:**

- **Definition:** Repeated cycles of nucleation and growth of new grains during hot deformation.
- **Mechanism:** Bulge nucleation at serrated grain boundaries → strain-induced boundary migration → necklace structure formation.
- **Microstructure:** Refined, equiaxed grains (e.g., 10-50 μm) with low dislocation density, maintained throughout hot working.
- **Limitation:** DRX dominates in **low SFE** metals (Cu, Ni, austenitic stainless steel, γ-Fe).

> **Engineering Application:** Controlled rolling of **HSLA (High Strength Low Alloy) steel** in the austenite range uses DRX to achieve a fine-grained ferrite transformation product, yielding excellent combinations of strength and toughness.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Recovery" with "Recrystallization"** — Many students describe both as "heat treatment removing defects." Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for not specifying the **nucleation of new strain-free grains** as the key recrystallization event.
>
> 2. **Missing the driving force** — Writing "grains grow" without stating *why* (grain boundary energy reduction) loses 1 mark per stage in 14-mark questions.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in Hall-Petch derivation** — Students often write $k_y$ without specifying Pa·m^(1/2). Examiners flag unit errors.
>
> 4. **No diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts** — Sketches showing elongated grains (cold work) or new equiaxed grains (recrystallized) earn **at least 1.5-2 bonus marks** in KTU evaluation.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the temperature ranges** — Every stage in annealing has a specific $T/T_m$ range. Writing generic "high temperature" loses marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the critical nucleus radius equation** $r^* = 2\gamma/\Delta G_v$ — This is a **favourite** in the 14-mark question. A derivation request without this equation typically scores at most 4-5 out of 7.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Board Exams):**

- ✅ **Microstructure** = internal architecture at the micro/nano scale (grains, boundaries, phases, dislocations, precipitates).
- ✅ **Cold working** = $T < 0.3 \, T_m$; increases dislocation density $\rho$; increases strength via Taylor equation $\Delta \sigma = \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$.
- ✅ **Hot working** = $T > 0.6 \, T_m$; dynamic recovery and dynamic recrystallization occur concurrently; no retained strain hardening.
- ✅ **Three annealing stages:** Recovery (defect annihilation), Recrystallization (new strain-free grains), Grain Growth (coarsening driven by boundary energy).
- ✅ **Recrystallization temperature:** $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (Kelvin); affected by prior strain, purity, and annealing time.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch relation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — derived from dislocation pile-up theory.
- ✅ **Inverse Hall-Petch** occurs below $d \approx 10\text{–}15$ nm due to grain boundary sliding dominance.
- ✅ **Grain growth kinetics:** $D^n - D_0^n = K_0 t \exp(-Q_{gg}/RT)$; typical $n = 2$.
- ✅ **Avrami equation:** $X = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$ with $n = 3\text{–}4$ for 3D recrystallization.
- ✅ **Creep deformation** (high T + constant load) is governed by $\dot{\epsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$.
- ✅ **Strengthening mechanisms hierarchy:** Solid solution → Strain hardening → Grain boundary → Precipitation → Composite (synergistic).
- ✅ **DRV dominates** in high SFE metals; **DRX dominates** in low SFE metals.
- ✅ **Fatigue damage** manifests as persistent slip bands (PSBs), extrusions, intrusions, and ultimately fatigue striations.
- ✅ **Martensitic transformation** (e.g., quenching of steel) is diffusionless, producing lath/plate morphology with extremely high dislocation density.

> **Final Exam Strategy:** Always link **microstructural feature → mechanism → property change → engineering application** in your answers. This four-link chain is what KTU examiners look for in 14-mark Part B responses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Length Scales Relevant for Materials and their Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT722 - ADVANCED MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Length Scales Relevant for Materials and their Characterization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a "Length Scale" in Materials Science? In materials science, a **length scale** is the characteristic dimension over which a particular physical, chemical, or structural phenomenon in a material becomes observable or dominant. The macroscopic behavior of any engineering component — be it an aircraft wing, a microchip, or a human bone — is fundamentally governed by processes occurring at length scales many orders of magnitude smaller than the component itself. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):** > A *length scale* is the characteristic physical dimension at which specific material features (such as atomic arrangements, defects, grains, or phases) dictate the observed bulk property of a material. It defines the boundary between regimes where different physical laws, theoretical models, and experimental characterization tools apply. ## 1.2 The Hierarchical Nature of Material Structure Materials exhibit a **multi-scale structural hierarchy**. To understand and engineer materials, one must appreciate how features at every level of this hierarchy contribute to the overall properties: | Length Scale | Typical Magnitude | Dominant Features | Governing Physics | |---|---|---|---| | **Sub-atomic** | $< 10^{-10}$ m | Electrons, protons, neutrons, quarks | Quantum mechanics | | **Atomic** | $\sim 10^{-10}$ m (1 Å) | Atomic nuclei, electronic orbitals, bonding | Quantum chemistry | | **Nano** | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-7}$ m | Nanoparticles, quantum dots, nanotubes | Quantum confinement, surface effects | | **Micro** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ m | Grains, dislocations, precipitates, microcracks | Solid mechanics, dislocation theory | | **Meso / Structural** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ m | Laminates, woven fibers, cellular structures | Continuum mechanics | | **Macro / Component** | $> 10^{-1}$ m | Engineering components, structures | Classical mechanics, FEM | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Russian Doll of Materials" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Russian Doll (Matryoshka):** > Imagine a material as a set of Russian nesting dolls. The outermost doll represents the macroscopic component you hold in your hand. Open it, and inside you find a smaller doll representing the microstructure (grains). Open that, and you see the atomic doll. Open that, and the innermost doll is the electron cloud. > **Every outer doll's behavior is dictated by the arrangement and interaction of the dolls inside it.** A crack that begins at the atomic scale (a broken bond) can propagate through grains (micro scale) and ultimately fracture the entire component (macro scale). The length scale is simply which "doll" you are currently examining. ## 1.4 Why Length Scales Matter for Characterization > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** *No single characterization technique can probe all length scales.* A transmission electron microscope (TEM) cannot see a bridge, and a ruler cannot see a dislocation. Hence, the choice of a characterization tool is dictated by the length scale of the feature of interest. **Standard Metrics to Remember:** - **1 Ångström (Å)** = $10^{-10}$ m = **0.1 nm** (typical atomic diameter) - **1 nanometer (nm)** = $10^{-9}$ m - **1 micrometer (µm)** = $10^{-6}$ m - **Bohr radius** $a_0 \approx 5.29 \times 10^{-11}$ m (reference atomic dimension) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Primary Length Scale Regimes ### Regime 1 — Electronic / Quantum Scale ($10^{-12}$ m to $10^{-9}$ m) - **What is happening:** Electrons orbit nuclei; chemical bonds form; energy bands emerge. - **Governing theory:** Schrödinger equation, density functional theory (DFT). - **Key metric:** Bohr radius, de Broglie wavelength, bond length. - **Property dominated:** Electrical conductivity, optical absorption, magnetism (intrinsic). ### Regime 2 — Atomic / Molecular Scale ($10^{-10}$ m to $10^{-9}$ m) - **What is happening:** Atoms assemble into specific crystal lattices (FCC, BCC, HCP, etc.) or amorphous networks. - **Governing theory:** Crystallography, solid-state physics. - **Key metric:** Lattice parameter $a$, interatomic spacing. - **Property dominated:** Elastic moduli, density, melting point (intrinsic). ### Regime 3 — Microstructural / Mesoscopic Scale ($10^{-9}$ m to $10^{-3}$ m) - **What is happening:** Atoms organize into grains, dislocations form, second-phase particles precipitate. - **Governing theory:** Dislocation theory, diffusion, thermodynamics of phase transformations. - **Key metrics:** Grain size $d$, dislocation density $\rho$, precipitate spacing $\lambda$. - **Property dominated:** Yield strength, hardness, toughness (extrinsic / process-dependent). ### Regime 4 — Macroscale / Continuum Scale ($> 10^{-3}$ m) - **What is happening:** The bulk material responds to external loads, heat, and electromagnetic fields as a continuous medium. - **Governing theory:** Continuum mechanics, thermodynamics. - **Key metrics:** Component dimensions, applied stress $\sigma$, strain $\varepsilon$. - **Property dominated:** Structural integrity, fatigue life, fracture toughness of components. ## 2.2 The Hall–Petch Relationship (A Classic Micro-to-Macro Link) One of the most important equations bridging length scales is the **Hall–Petch relation**, which connects the grain size (micro scale) to the yield strength (macro property): $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$$ Where: - $\sigma_y$ = yield stress (Pa) - $\sigma_0$ = friction stress of the lattice (Pa) - $k_y$ = Hall–Petch coefficient (Pa·m$^{1/2}$) - $d$ = average grain diameter (m) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** This single equation demonstrates that a property measured at the macro scale ($\sigma_y$) is *inversely proportional to the square root of a microstructural feature* ($d$). Halving the grain size increases strength significantly — a direct length-scale-dependent property. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch: grain size → yield strength | $\sigma$ in Pa, $d$ in m, $k_y$ in Pa·m$^{1/2}$ | | 2 | $\rho_d = N / V$ | Dislocation density: total line length per unit volume | m$^{-2}$ | | 3 | $\lambda = \left( \tfrac{4r}{3f} \right) (1-f)$ | Inter-particle spacing (precipitates) | $r$ = particle radius, $f$ = volume fraction | | 4 | $R = \lambda / (2 \sin\theta)$ | Bragg's Law (X-ray diffraction) | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\theta$ = Bragg angle | | 5 | $d_{hkl} = a / \sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | Interplanar spacing in cubic crystal | $a$ = lattice parameter | | 6 | $V_{\text{atom}} = a^3 / 4$ (FCC) | Atomic packing volume (FCC) | $a$ = lattice parameter | | 7 | $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$ | Avogadro's number (ref. for atomic scale counting) | mol$^{-1}$ | | 8 | $a_0 \approx 5.29 \times 10^{-11}$ m | Bohr radius (atomic dimension) | m | | 9 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain (macro scale) | Dimensionless | | 10 | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Engineering stress (macro scale) | Pa = N/m$^2$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world KTU-relevant engineering problems, the length scale of a phenomenon determines the *design strategy*. A nano-electronic device requires atomically precise fabrication (e.g., molecular beam epitaxy), while a civil engineering beam is designed using continuum stress-strain analysis. Selecting the appropriate scale is the first step in any materials engineering decision. ## 2.4 The "Structure-Property-Processing-Performance" Paradigm > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every material's performance is a function of its **processing**, which controls its **structure** across all length scales, which in turn determines its **properties**. > > - Processing (heat treatment, rolling, casting) → > - Structure (atomic, micro, macro features) → > - Properties (mechanical, electrical, thermal) → > - Performance (in-service behavior) > > *The length scale of a "feature" you want to control dictates which processing route and characterization tool you must use.* # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Atomic Packing Factor of an FCC Crystal The **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)** is a fundamental length-scale metric that quantifies the fraction of volume in a unit cell occupied by atoms. **Step 1: Identify atoms per FCC unit cell.** In a face-centered cubic cell: - 8 corner atoms, each shared among 8 cells → contribution $= 8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1$ atom - 6 face-centered atoms, each shared between 2 cells → contribution $= 6 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 3$ atoms - Total atoms per unit cell $= 1 + 3 = 4$ atoms. **Step 2: Establish the geometric relationship between atomic radius $R$ and lattice parameter $a$.** In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**. The face diagonal length is: $$L_{\text{diagonal}} = a\sqrt{2}$$ Along this diagonal, the atoms are arranged as: radius + diameter + radius $= R + 2R + R = 4R$. Therefore: $$4R = a\sqrt{2}$$ Solving for $R$: $$R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$$ **Step 3: Compute the volume of atoms in one unit cell.** Volume of a single atom (sphere): $$V_{\text{atom}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$$ Total atomic volume in cell: $$V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 = \frac{16}{3} \pi R^3$$ **Step 4: Substitute $R$ in terms of $a$.** Using $R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$: $$V_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{16}{3} \pi \left( \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4} \right)^3$$ $$= \frac{16}{3} \pi \cdot \frac{a^3 \cdot 2\sqrt{2}}{64}$$ $$= \frac{16 \pi a^3 \cdot 2\sqrt{2}}{3 \times 64}$$ $$= \frac{32\sqrt{2} \, \pi a^3}{192}$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{2} \, \pi a^3}{6}$$ **Step 5: Compute the volume of the unit cell.** $$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3$$ **Step 6: Compute the APF.** $$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\tfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\pi a^3}{6}}{a^3}$$ $$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7405 \;(74.05\%)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** 74.05% of the unit cell volume is occupied by atoms — the highest possible packing efficiency for identical spheres in nature. This is a *purely geometric* length-scale property of the FCC structure. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Hall–Petch Slope and Its Length-Scale Limit The Hall–Petch equation can be derived from dislocation pile-up theory. Consider a grain of diameter $d$ containing a pile-up of $n$ dislocations against a grain boundary. **Step 1: The number of dislocations in a pile-up.** The shear stress $\tau$ acting on the leading dislocation of a pile-up of length $L$ (where $L \approx d/2$) produces a stress concentration at the tip. The number of dislocations in the pile-up is: $$n = \frac{k \pi d \tau}{2 G b}$$ where $G$ is the shear modulus, $b$ is the Burgers vector, and $k$ is a constant of order unity. **Step 2: Stress concentration at the pile-up tip.** The leading dislocation experiences a concentrated stress: $$\tau_{\text{tip}} = n \tau = \frac{k \pi d \tau^2}{2 G b}$$ **Step 3: Yielding criterion.** Yielding occurs when $\tau_{\text{tip}}$ reaches a critical value $\tau_c$ needed to activate slip in the adjacent grain. Setting $\tau_{\text{tip}} = \tau_c$: $$\frac{k \pi d \tau_y^2}{2 G b} = \tau_c$$ **Step 4: Solve for $\tau_y$ in terms of $d$.** $$\tau_y^2 = \frac{2 G b \, \tau_c}{k \pi d}$$ $$\tau_y = \sqrt{\frac{2 G b \, \tau_c}{k \pi}} \cdot d^{-1/2}$$ **Step 5: Convert to normal stress and write in Hall–Petch form.** Using $\sigma_y = 2\tau_y$ and consolidating constants: $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$$ where $k_y = 2 \sqrt{\frac{2 G b \, \tau_c}{k \pi}}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lower-bound limit:** The Hall–Petch relation breaks down when $d$ approaches the nanometer regime (typically $d < 20$ nm). Below this, the concept of a dislocation pile-up ceases to exist, and materials can even show *inverse* Hall–Petch behavior (strength decreases with further grain refinement). This is a critical length-scale transition. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Length-Scale Conversion **Problem:** A steel specimen has a measured yield strength of 350 MPa, and a Hall–Petch coefficient $k_y = 0.65$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$. The friction stress $\sigma_0 = 50$ MPa. Determine the average grain diameter. **Step 1: Apply the Hall–Petch relation.** $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$$ $$350 = 50 + 0.65 \, d^{-1/2}$$ **Step 2: Isolate $d^{-1/2}$.** $$d^{-1/2} = \frac{350 - 50}{0.65} = \frac{300}{0.65} \approx 461.54 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$ **Step 3: Invert to find $d^{1/2}$.** $$d^{1/2} = \frac{1}{461.54} \approx 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$ **Step 4: Square to find $d$.** $$d = (2.166 \times 10^{-3})^2 \approx 4.69 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 4.69 \; \mu\text{m}}$$ **Step 5: Length-scale identification.** This grain size falls in the **microstructural scale** ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ m), confirming the relationship is a micro-to-macro property link. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Length-Scale Map (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["MACRO SCALE
Greater than 0.1 m
Engineering components and structures"] B["MESO SCALE
0.001 m to 0.1 m
Laminates, fibers, cellular foams"] C["MICRO SCALE
1 micrometer to 1000 micrometer
Grains, phases, dislocations"] D["NANO SCALE
1 nm to 1000 nm
Nanoparticles, quantum dots, nanotubes"] E["ATOMIC SCALE
0.1 nm to 1 nm
Atoms, crystal lattices, bonds"] F["ELECTRONIC SCALE
Less than 0.1 nm
Electrons, nuclei, orbitals"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F G["CHARACTERIZATION TOOLS"] -.-> A H["Optical microscopy, SEM, tensile testing"] -.-> A I["Optical microscopy, SEM"] -.-> B J["Optical microscopy, SEM, EBSD"] -.-> C K["TEM, AFM, XRD, SAXS"] -.-> D L["TEM, XRD, EXAFS, STM"] -.-> E M["DFT, spectroscopy, quantum chemistry"] -.-> F ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Each rectangle is a length-scale regime. As you move downward, the dimension shrinks by orders of magnitude. The right-side annotations indicate which experimental techniques probe each regime. **Notice how techniques overlap at the boundaries** — this is the realistic case where one tool can probe two adjacent scales. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Structure-Property-Performance Link ```mermaid flowchart LR P["PROCESSING
Heat treatment, casting,
rolling, deposition, sintering"] S["STRUCTURE
Atomic, micro, macro
features across all length scales"] PR["PROPERTIES
Mechanical, thermal,
electrical, optical, magnetic"] PF["PERFORMANCE
Service life, reliability,
functionality of the component"] P --> S S --> PR PR --> PF C1["LENGTH-SCALE-AWARE
CHARACTERIZATION"] -.-> S C2["SIMULATION AND
MODELING"] -.-> S C3["DESIGN
CONSTRAINTS"] -.-> PR P -->|input parameters| S C1 -->|feedback| P ``` **Reading the architecture:** - The **solid arrows** show the unidirectional cause-effect chain: *Processing → Structure → Properties → Performance*. - The **dashed arrows** show feedback loops: characterization feeds back to processing, and design constraints inform property targets. - The *central role* of "Length-Scale-Aware Characterization" emphasizes that one cannot optimize the chain without verifying the structure at the correct length scale. --- ## 4.3 Length-Scale vs. Characterization Technique Matrix ```mermaid graph TD subgraph QUANTUM["QUANTUM AND ATOMIC REGIME"] Q1["X ray diffraction XRD"] Q2["Transmission electron microscopy TEM"] Q3["Scanning tunneling microscopy STM"] Q4["Density functional theory DFT"] end subgraph NANO["NANO REGIME"] N1["Atomic force microscopy AFM"] N2["Small angle X ray scattering SAXS"] N3["High resolution TEM"] N4["Electron energy loss spectroscopy EELS"] end subgraph MICRO["MICRO REGIME"] M1["Scanning electron microscopy SEM"] M2["Optical microscopy"] M3["Electron backscatter diffraction EBSD"] M4["Focused ion beam FIB"] end subgraph MACRO["MACRO REGIME"] A1["Universal testing machine UTM"] A2["Impact testing Charpy Izod"] A3["Hardness testing Vickers Rockwell"] A4["Non destructive testing NDT"] end QUANTUM --> NANO NANO --> MICRO MICRO --> MACRO ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway from the Matrix:** The progression from quantum (XRD, DFT) → nano (AFM, SAXS) → micro (SEM, optical) → macro (UTM) is *not arbitrary* — it directly mirrors the length-scale hierarchy of materials structure. A KTU examiner will expect you to **match a technique to a length scale** without hesitation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "length scale" in the context of materials science. List the four major length-scale regimes and give one characteristic feature of each.** **Model Answer:** A *length scale* is the characteristic physical dimension at which a specific material feature dictates bulk properties. The four major regimes are: 1. **Atomic scale** ($\sim 10^{-10}$ m): atoms and crystal lattices. 2. **Micro scale** ($10^{-9}$ to $10^{-4}$ m): grains, dislocations, precipitates. 3. **Meso scale** ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ m): fiber arrangements, laminates, cellular structures. 4. **Macro scale** ($> 10^{-1}$ m): engineering components and structures. Each regime is governed by different physical models — quantum at atomic, dislocation theory at micro, continuum mechanics at macro. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four regimes with features.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is it impossible to characterize all material features with a single instrument? Illustrate with two examples.** **Model Answer:** No single instrument can probe all length scales because each technique has a fundamental **resolution limit** dictated by the wavelength of the probe (electrons, photons, ions, or mechanical tips) and the interaction volume with the sample. - **Example 1:** A standard optical microscope is limited to $\sim 200$ nm resolution by the wavelength of visible light. It cannot image individual atoms. - **Example 2:** A tensile testing machine (UTM) measures the *bulk* stress-strain response of a specimen but provides no information on grain boundaries or dislocations. Hence, materials characterization is fundamentally a **multi-technique exercise** that spans the entire length-scale hierarchy. *(3 marks: 1 mark for principle + 2 marks for two examples.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the hierarchical length-scale structure of materials. Explain how properties at the macro scale emerge from features at the atomic and micro scales. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Atomic Packing Factor (APF) for an FCC crystal. State clearly the geometric assumptions used. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Hierarchical Structure of Materials:** Materials exhibit a **multi-level structural hierarchy** with each level influencing the next: | Level | Length Scale | Feature | |---|---|---| | 1 | Sub-atomic / Electronic | Electrons, energy bands | | 2 | Atomic | Crystal structure, atomic bonding | | 3 | Nano | Nanoclusters, quantum confinement | | 4 | Micro | Grains, dislocations, precipitates | | 5 | Meso | Fiber-matrix, laminates | | 6 | Macro | Engineering component | **Sketch (Text Representation):** ``` [MACRO] Engineering component | [MESO] Fibrous laminate | [MICRO] Grain (with grain boundaries) | [NANO] Precipitate / nanoparticle | [ATOMIC] Unit cell with atoms | [ELECTRONIC] Energy band / electron cloud ``` **Property Emergence — A Causal Chain:** - At the **electronic scale**, the arrangement of electrons in energy bands determines whether a material is a conductor, semiconductor, or insulator. - At the **atomic scale**, the type of bonding (metallic, ionic, covalent) governs melting point, elastic modulus, and density. - At the **micro scale**, grain size controls yield strength (Hall–Petch), dislocation density controls ductility, and precipitate distribution controls hardness. - At the **macro scale**, the bulk material responds to applied loads, and its in-service performance is the *integrated consequence* of all underlying length scales. > **Concluding remark (for 7-mark answer):** Macro properties such as yield strength, fracture toughness, and electrical conductivity are *not* independent of the smaller scales — they are the cumulative result of features that originate at the atomic and micro levels. This is why engineering a material requires control across the entire hierarchy. *(7 marks: 2 for sketch + 2 for hierarchical list + 3 for property emergence discussion.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Derivation of APF for FCC:** **Assumption:** Atoms are rigid, non-overlapping spheres that touch each other along the face diagonal of the cube. **Step 1: Atoms per unit cell.** $$N = 8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} + 6 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 4 \text{ atoms}$$ **Step 2: Geometric relation.** In FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal: $$4R = a\sqrt{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$$ **Step 3: Volume of atoms.** $$V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi R^3$$ Substituting $R = \tfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$: $$V_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{16}{3}\pi \left(\frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,a^3}{64} = \frac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}$$ **Step 4: Volume of unit cell.** $$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3$$ **Step 5: Atomic Packing Factor.** $$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7405$$ $$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = 74.05\%}$$ **Geometric assumption used:** Atoms touch along the face diagonal, not along the cube edge. *(7 marks: 1 for stating assumption + 2 for atoms/cell + 2 for geometric relation + 2 for final APF.)* --- ### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Hall–Petch relationship. Discuss its physical origin and the length-scale regime in which it is valid. What happens at very small grain sizes? **(b) [7 Marks]** A copper specimen has a yield strength of 180 MPa. Given $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa and $k_y = 0.18$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, calculate the average grain diameter. If the grain size is reduced to 5 µm, what will be the new yield strength? Identify the length scale of the grains in both cases. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Hall–Petch Relationship:** $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$$ This equation states that the yield strength $\sigma_y$ of a polycrystalline metal increases as the grain size $d$ decreases. The plot of $\sigma_y$ versus $d^{-1/2}$ is a straight line with slope $k_y$ and intercept $\sigma_0$. **Physical Origin (Dislocation Pile-Up Theory):** In a coarse-grained metal, dislocations glide on slip planes and pile up against grain boundaries. The grain boundary acts as an obstacle. A pile-up of $n$ dislocations produces a stress concentration at the tip: $$\tau_{\text{tip}} = n \cdot \tau \propto d^{1/2} \cdot \tau$$ Yielding occurs when this concentrated stress activates slip in the adjacent grain. Solving for the applied stress $\tau$ required for yielding: $$\tau_y \propto d^{-1/2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$$ **Length-Scale Validity:** - The relationship is valid in the **microstructural regime** ($d \approx 1\;\mu\text{m}$ to $100\;\mu\text{m}$). - It fails at very small grain sizes (typically $d < 20$ nm) because dislocation pile-ups cannot form — there are simply not enough dislocations within each grain to produce a meaningful pile-up. - Below this threshold, materials exhibit the **inverse Hall–Petch effect**: yield strength *decreases* with further grain refinement due to grain-boundary sliding and diffusion-controlled deformation mechanisms. *(7 marks: 2 for equation + 2 for physical origin + 2 for validity range + 1 for inverse behavior.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** - $\sigma_y = 180$ MPa - $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa - $k_y = 0.18$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ **Step 1: Find initial grain diameter $d_1$.** $$180 = 25 + 0.18 \, d_1^{-1/2}$$ $$d_1^{-1/2} = \frac{180 - 25}{0.18} = \frac{155}{0.18} = 861.11 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$ $$d_1^{1/2} = \frac{1}{861.11} = 1.161 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$ $$d_1 = (1.161 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.349 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d_1 \approx 1.35 \; \mu\text{m}}$$ **Step 2: Find new yield strength for $d_2 = 5\;\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m.** $$\sigma_{y,2} = 25 + 0.18 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2}$$ $$(5 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2.236 \times 10^{-3}} = 447.21 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$ $$\sigma_{y,2} = 25 + 0.18 \times 447.21 = 25 + 80.50$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{y,2} \approx 105.5 \text{ MPa}}$$ **Step 3: Length-scale identification.** - Initial grain size $d_1 \approx 1.35\;\mu\text{m}$ lies at the **nano-to-micro boundary** ($10^{-6}$ m range). - New grain size $d_2 = 5\;\mu\text{m}$ lies in the **micro scale** regime. > **Important Note:** Reducing the grain size from $5\;\mu\text{m}$ to $\sim 1.35\;\mu\text{m}$ has *increased* the yield strength from 105.5 MPa to 180 MPa — a demonstration of length-scale-driven property control. *(7 marks: 2 for $d_1$ calculation + 2 for $\sigma_{y,2}$ calculation + 2 for length-scale identification + 1 for final remark.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Mixing up length scales:** Students often write "grain size" without specifying whether they mean micro or nano. Always state the *order of magnitude* explicitly. > 2. **Hall–Petch units confusion:** The coefficient $k_y$ has units of **Pa·m$^{1/2}$** — not Pa, not m. Forgetting this leads to incorrect numerical answers. > 3. **APF derivation error:** A common mistake is assuming atoms touch along the cube edge. In FCC they touch along the **face diagonal** ($4R = a\sqrt{2}$), while in BCC they touch along the **body diagonal** ($4R = a\sqrt{3}$). > 4. **Forgetting to identify the length scale** in numerical problems. KTU examiners award marks for explicitly stating which regime the answer belongs to. > 5. **Confusing "characterization" with "testing":** Characterization = observing structure (microscopy, diffraction). Testing = measuring response (tensile, hardness). These are different but complementary. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** > > - Length scales span **12+ orders of magnitude** from sub-atomic ($<10^{-12}$ m) to macroscopic ($>10^{-1}$ m). > - The four key regimes: **Electronic → Atomic → Microstructural → Macroscale**. > - **Hall–Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — links micro grain size to macro yield strength. > - **Hall–Petch breaks down** below $\sim 20$ nm grain size (inverse Hall–Petch regime). > - **APF of FCC** = 74.05%, derived using face-diagonal contact ($4R = a\sqrt{2}$). > - **Characterization tools are scale-specific:** XRD/TEM for atomic, SEM for micro, UTM for macro. > - The **Structure-Property-Processing-Performance** paradigm: every macro property originates from features at smaller scales. > - Always state the **length scale** explicitly in your answer — it is a KTU marking expectation. > - Standard metrics: 1 Å = $10^{-10}$ m, Bohr radius $a_0 \approx 5.29 \times 10^{-11}$ m. > - The **Russian Doll analogy** is an excellent way to remember hierarchical structure during viva voce.

topics.title: Types of Metal Alloys, Processing and Applications of Metal Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT722 - ADVANCED MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Metal Alloys, Processing and Applications of Metal Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Metal Alloy?

A **metal alloy** is a metallic substance composed of two or more elements, where at least one element is a metal, and the resulting material exhibits metallic properties (e.g., electrical conductivity, ductility, luster) superior or different from its constituent elements. Alloys are engineered to enhance strength, corrosion resistance, hardness, toughness, or specific functional properties that pure metals cannot provide alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "An alloy is a substance having metallic properties and being composed of two or more chemical elements, of which at least one is a metal. The components of alloys are termed as *parent metal* (base metal), *alloying elements*, and *impurities*."

### 1.2 Fundamental Classification of Alloys

Metal alloys are classified based on the number of constituent phases present in their microstructure at ambient conditions:

- **Single-Phase Alloys (Homogeneous Alloys):** Exhibit only one phase throughout; uniform microstructure. Example: Copper-zinc brass in α-phase region (less than **35% Zn**).
- **Multi-Phase Alloys (Heterogeneous Alloys):** Consist of two or more distinct phases. Example: Steel containing α-ferrite and Fe₃C (cementite), or eutectic alloys like Pb-Sn solder.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Metal Cocktail"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a pure metal like **plain water (H₂O)**. It has fixed properties. Now imagine you dissolve **salt, sugar, and lemon** in it to make *lemonade* — the resulting drink has entirely new taste, color, and properties that water alone never had. Similarly, an **alloy** is the "lemonade" of metallurgy — combining a base metal (water) with alloying elements (salt, sugar, lemon) creates a new material with tailor-made properties. Just as you cannot recover the salt/sugar by simple filtering, alloying permanently alters the metallic structure at the atomic level.

### 1.4 The Five Universal Reasons for Alloying

Engineers never alloy metals randomly. There are **five primary motives** behind alloy design, all highly emphasized by KTU examiners:

1. **Strengthening** — Solid solution strengthening, precipitation hardening, grain refinement (e.g., adding C to Fe to make steel).
2. **Corrosion Resistance** — Adding Cr to Fe creates stainless steel (Cr > **10.5%**).
3. **Improved Fabricability** — Improving castability, machinability, weldability.
4. **Thermal Stability** — Maintaining properties at elevated temperatures (e.g., Ni-based superalloys for jet engines).
5. **Cost Reduction** — Replacing expensive metals with cheaper alternatives (e.g., Zn in Cu to make brass).

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Referenced in Alloy Design

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** | Atomic packing calculations |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Diffusion coefficient |
| Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Thermodynamic calculations |
| Atomic Packing Factor (FCC) | APF | **0.74** | Theoretical density |
| Atomic Packing Factor (BCC) | APF | **0.68** | Theoretical density |
| Atomic Packing Factor (HCP) | APF | **0.74** | Theoretical density |
| Iron α→γ Transition Temp. | $T_{c}$ | **912 °C (or 1183 K)** | Steel heat treatment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Metal Alloys

Metal alloys are broadly classified into **Ferrous** (iron-based) and **Non-Ferrous** (non-iron-based) categories. Each category has sub-divisions based on composition and properties.

#### 2.1.1 Ferrous Alloys (Iron-Based)

Ferrous alloys account for over **85% by weight** of all metal production worldwide. Iron is the base metal, and alloying elements include C, Cr, Ni, Mn, Mo, V, Si, W, Co.

**A. Steels** (Fe-C alloys with C < **2.14 wt%**)

- **Plain Carbon Steels** — Classified further:
  - **Low Carbon Steel** (C: 0.05–0.25%) — Mild steel; automotive body panels, wires, structural shapes.
  - **Medium Carbon Steel** (C: 0.25–0.60%) — Railway tracks, gears, crankshafts.
  - **High Carbon Steel** (C: 0.60–1.0%) — Springs, blades, chisels, cutting tools.
  - **Ultra-High Carbon Steel** (C: 1.0–2.14%) — Axes, punches, knives.

- **Alloy Steels** — Steels with specified additions beyond C (typically > **8 wt% total alloying** for high-alloy steels).
  - **Low-Alloy Steels** — Total alloying < **8%** (e.g., 4140, 4340).
  - **High-Alloy Steels** — Total alloying > **8%** (e.g., stainless steels).

- **Stainless Steels** (Cr > **10.5%** for passivation):
  - **Austenitic (300 series, e.g., 304, 316):** Non-magnetic, FCC, excellent corrosion resistance. Used in kitchenware, chemical plants.
  - **Ferritic (400 series, e.g., 430):** Magnetic, BCC, moderate corrosion resistance. Used in automotive trim, exhaust systems.
  - **Martensitic (e.g., 410, 420):** Hardenable by heat treatment. Used in cutlery, surgical instruments.
  - **Duplex (e.g., 2205):** Mixed FCC+BCC. Used in marine, oil & gas.
  - **Precipitation-Hardening (e.g., 17-4 PH):** Ultra-high strength. Used in aerospace.

**B. Cast Irons** (Fe-C alloys with C > **2.14 wt%**, typically 2.5–4.5% C + 1–3% Si)

- **White Cast Iron** — Carbon as Fe₃C (cementite); hard, brittle; abrasion-resistant.
- **Gray Cast Iron** — Carbon as graphite flakes; excellent damping, machinable; engine blocks.
- **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** — Spheroidal graphite via Mg/Ce treatment; high ductility.
- **Malleable Cast Iron** — Heat-treated white iron with temper carbon rosettes.
- **High-Alloy Cast Irons** — Ni-Hard, high-Cr irons for wear resistance.

#### 2.1.2 Non-Ferrous Alloys (Non-Iron-Based)

**A. Aluminum Alloys** — Lightweight (ρ ≈ **2.7 g/cm³**), high strength-to-weight ratio, excellent corrosion resistance due to Al₂O₃ layer.

- **Wrought Alloys (4-digit designation, e.g., 2024, 6061, 7075):**
  - **2xxx series** — Al-Cu (e.g., 2024-T3); aircraft structures.
  - **6xxx series** — Al-Mg-Si (e.g., 6061-T6); architectural, automotive.
  - **7xxx series** — Al-Zn-Mg-Cu (e.g., 7075-T6); aerospace, military.
- **Cast Alloys (3-digit + decimal, e.g., 356.0, 319.0):** Engine cylinder heads, pistons.

**B. Copper Alloys** — Excellent electrical/thermal conductivity (ρ ≈ **8.96 g/cm³**).

- **Brasses** — Cu-Zn (e.g., Cartridge brass 70/30, Muntz metal 60/40).
- **Bronzes** — Cu-Sn (historically); now Cu-Al (aluminum bronze), Cu-Si (silicon bronze).
- **Cupronickels** — Cu-Ni (e.g., 70/30 for marine heat exchangers).
- **Beryllium Copper** — Cu-Be (age-hardenable); springs, non-sparking tools.

**C. Titanium Alloys** — Biocompatible, high strength, ρ ≈ **4.5 g/cm³**.

- **α alloys** (e.g., Ti-5Al-2.5Sn) — Weldable, creep-resistant.
- **β alloys** (e.g., Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al) — Heat-treatable, formable.
- **α+β alloys** (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V, the "workhorse") — Aerospace, biomedical implants.

**D. Nickel Alloys** — High-temperature, corrosion-resistant.

- **Ni-Cu** (e.g., Monel 400) — Marine, chemical.
- **Ni-Cr** (e.g., Inconel 625, 718) — Jet engines, nuclear reactors.
- **Ni-Fe** (e.g., Invar 36, with 64% Fe) — Low thermal expansion; precision instruments.

**E. Magnesium Alloys** — Ultra-lightweight (ρ ≈ **1.74 g/cm³**).

- **AZ91D** (Mg-Al-Zn) — Die-cast automotive parts, electronics housings.
- **WE43, ZE41** — High-temperature aerospace and racing.

**F. Refractory Metals** — W, Mo, Ta, Nb — High melting points (W: **3422 °C**, highest of all metals).

### 2.2 Processing of Metal Alloys

The processing route fundamentally determines the final microstructure and thus the mechanical properties of any alloy.

#### 2.2.1 Primary Forming Processes

**A. Casting** — Molten alloy poured into a mold, solidified.
- **Sand Casting** — Low-cost, large parts (engine blocks).
- **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax)** — Precision parts, aerospace turbine blades.
- **Die Casting** — High-volume production (Zn, Al, Mg alloys).
- **Continuous Casting** — Slabs, billets for rolling mills.

**B. Forming (Deformation Processing)** — Plastic deformation below melting point.

- **Rolling** — Hot/cold; produces plates, sheets, rails.
- **Forging** — Open-die or closed-die; high-strength components (crankshafts, connecting rods).
- **Extrusion** — Direct or indirect; produces long constant cross-sections (Al window frames).
- **Drawing** — Wire production.
- **Stamping/Pressing** — Sheet metal parts (car body panels).

#### 2.2.2 Heat Treatment Processes (Specifically for Steels)

**A. Annealing** — Heating above critical temperature, holding, slow cooling.
- Sub-types: Full anneal, Process anneal, Stress relief, Spheroidization.

**B. Normalizing** — Air cooling from above $A_{c3}$ — refines grain structure.

**C. Quenching** — Rapid cooling (water/oil/polymer) from austenitizing temperature — produces martensite.

**D. Tempering** — Reheating quenched steel to **150–650 °C** — reduces brittleness, improves toughness.

**E. Austempering** — Quench into molten salt bath (≈ 260–400 °C), hold → **Bainite** (no tempering needed).

**F. Martempering** — Interrupted quench → uniform temperature → air cool to martensite (minimizes distortion).

**G. Precipitation Hardening (Age Hardening)** — Solution treatment → Quench → Aging. Used for Al-Cu, Cu-Be, Ni-Al systems.

#### 2.2.3 Powder Metallurgy (P/M)

Used when casting is impossible (e.g., refractory metals, cemented carbides, porous bearings).
Steps: **Powder production → Blending → Compaction (cold press, typically at 200–1000 MPa) → Sintering (heating below melting point, e.g., 70–90% of $T_m$) → Optional secondary operations.**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU board examiners frequently test these formulas in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| Formula | Expression | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atomic Packing Factor | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}}$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical density |
| Theoretical Density | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_c \cdot N_A}$ | $\rho$ (g/cm³), $n$ atoms/unit cell, $A$ atomic mass (g/mol), $V_c$ cell volume (cm³) | Alloy density estimation |
| Diffusion (Fick's First Law) | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $J$ (atoms/m²·s), $D$ (m²/s), $C$ (atoms/m³) | Heat treatment, carburizing |
| Arrhenius Diffusivity | $D = D_0 \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | $D_0$ (m²/s), $Q_d$ (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ (K) | Diffusion-controlled processes |
| Yield Strength (Hall-Petch) | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ | $\sigma_0, k_y$ constants, $d$ = grain diameter (m) | Grain-size strengthening |
| Solid Solution Strength. | $\sigma = \sigma_0 + k \cdot X^{n}$ | $X$ = atom fraction solute | Alloy strengthening |
| % Cold Work | $\%\text{CW} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | $A_0$ original, $A_f$ final area | Rolling/drawing |
| Recrystallization Temp. | $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (K) | $T_m$ = melting point in K | Annealing process design |
| Young's Modulus (composite, Voigt) | $E_c = V_1 E_1 + V_2 E_2$ | $V_i$ = volume fractions, $E_i$ = moduli | Composite/alloy estimate |
| Young's Modulus (Reuss) | $E_c^{-1} = V_1 E_1^{-1} + V_2 E_2^{-1}$ | Same as above | Composite lower bound |
| Rule of Mixtures (Density) | $\rho_c = \Sigma V_i \rho_i$ | $V_i$ = volume fraction, $\rho_i$ = density | Alloy density |
| Hardness Conversion (approx.) | $\text{HV} \approx 0.95 \, \text{HB}$ | Vickers vs Brinell | Material property check |
| Heat Capacity (Dulong-Petit) | $C_v \approx 3R = 24.94$ J/(mol·K) | At high $T$ | Solid-state estimation |

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Aerospace** demands the 7075 Al alloy (T6) and Ti-6Al-4V for wing spars, fuselage skins — weight savings of 1 kg saves **~3000 liters of fuel per year** per aircraft.
- **Biomedical implants** rely on Ti-6Al-4V ELI and Co-Cr alloys for biocompatibility and osseointegration.
- **Power plants** use Inconel 718 for turbine blades operating at > 700 °C.
- **Automotive crash structures** use DP (dual-phase) and TRIP (transformation-induced plasticity) advanced high-strength steels (AHSS).
- **Marine environments** require Cu-Ni alloys (70/30) and super-duplex stainless steel (2507) for propellers, hulls, heat exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Density of an FCC Alloy

Consider a binary FCC alloy of two elements A and B, with average atomic mass $\bar{A}$ and atomic radius $r$. In a unit FCC cell, the lattice parameter is:

$$
a = 2r\sqrt{2}
$$

The number of atoms per unit cell in FCC is **n = 4**. The volume of the unit cell is:

$$
V_c = a^3 = (2r\sqrt{2})^3 = 16 r^3 \sqrt{2}
$$

The theoretical density is therefore:

$$
\rho_{\text{th}} = \frac{n \cdot \bar{A}}{V_c \cdot N_A} = \frac{4 \cdot \bar{A}}{16 r^3 \sqrt{2} \cdot N_A}
$$

Simplifying the constants:

$$
\rho_{\text{th}} = \frac{\bar{A}}{4\sqrt{2} \, r^3 N_A}
$$

Substituting the typical FCC atomic radius $r$ (e.g., for Cu, $r = 1.28 \times 10^{-8}$ cm, $\bar{A} = 63.55$ g/mol):

$$
\rho_{\text{Cu, FCC}} = \frac{4 \times 63.55}{(2 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-8})^3 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot 6.022 \times 10^{23}}
$$

Numerator: $4 \times 63.55 = 254.20$ g/mol.
Denominator: $(2.56 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 1.6777 \times 10^{-23}$ cm³.
Denominator total: $1.6777 \times 10^{-23} \times 1.4142 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 14.276$ cm³/mol.

$$
\rho_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{254.20}{14.276} \approx 8.91 \;\; \text{g/cm}^3
$$

> **Note:** The handbook value of Cu density is **8.96 g/cm³**; the small difference is due to the assumption of hard-sphere atomic contact. This confirms the validity of the formula.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hall-Petch Strengthening Equation

Consider a polycrystalline material with average grain diameter $d$. The yield stress depends on dislocation pile-ups at grain boundaries. For a pile-up of $n$ dislocations, the resolved shear stress at the boundary is:

$$
\tau_{\text{pile-up}} = n \tau_0
$$

The number of dislocations in a pile-up is proportional to the square root of grain size:

$$
n \propto \sqrt{d}
$$

The applied stress needed to propagate yielding into the next grain is:

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + k' \cdot \sqrt{d}
$$

Converting from shear to tensile and using $d^{-1/2}$ for convenience:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** Halving the grain diameter $d$ increases yield strength by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is why **grain refinement** is one of the most important strengthening mechanisms in metallurgy.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Radius for Recrystallization Nucleation

For a spherical nucleus of radius $r$ in a deformed matrix, the total free energy change is:

$$
\Delta G = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma
$$

where $\Delta G_v$ is the volumetric free energy change (negative, driving force) and $\gamma$ is the surface energy per unit area. Setting $\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = 4\pi r^2 \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \gamma = 0
$$

Solving for the critical radius:

$$
r_c = -\frac{2\gamma}{\Delta G_v}
$$

The critical activation energy is then:

$$
\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^3}{3 (\Delta G_v)^2}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** Cold-worked alloys with high stored energy (large $\vert \Delta G_v \vert$) have a small $r_c$ — making recrystallization spontaneous. The empirical **0.4 $T_m$ rule** ensures sufficient atomic mobility for recrystallization to occur in a reasonable time.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rule of Mixtures for Alloy Density

Consider a binary alloy of components A and B with mass fractions $w_A$ and $w_B$ and densities $\rho_A, \rho_B$. The volume of 1 kg of alloy is:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \frac{w_A}{\rho_A} + \frac{w_B}{\rho_B}
$$

The density of the alloy is then:

$$
\rho_{\text{alloy}} = \frac{1}{\frac{w_A}{\rho_A} + \frac{w_B}{\rho_B}}
$$

**Example:** 70/30 brass (70% Cu, 30% Zn by mass).
$\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $\rho_{\text{Zn}} = 7.14$ g/cm³.

$$
\rho_{\text{brass}} = \frac{1}{\frac{0.70}{8.96} + \frac{0.30}{7.14}} = \frac{1}{0.0781 + 0.0420} = \frac{1}{0.1201} = 8.33 \;\; \text{g/cm}^3
$$

The handbook value is approximately **8.5 g/cm³** — the discrepancy is due to lattice distortion in the real alloy.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Alloys for Engineering Applications

| Application | Required Property | Best Alloy Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft wing skin | High strength/weight | Al 7075-T6 | $\sigma_y$ = 503 MPa, $\rho$ = 2.81 g/cm³ |
| Jet engine turbine blade | Creep resistance > 700 °C | Inconel 718 (Ni-base) | $\gamma'$ + $\gamma''$ precipitates |
| Hip implant | Biocompatibility + fatigue | Ti-6Al-4V ELI | Passive TiO₂ layer, low modulus |
| Engine block (car) | Damping + castability | Gray cast iron | Graphite flakes dampen vibration |
| Marine propeller | Corrosion in seawater | Ni-Al bronze (Cu-9Al-5Ni-4Fe) | Cavitation + corrosion resistance |
| Cutting tool tip | Hardness at red heat | WC-Co (cemented carbide) | Hardness > 1500 HV at 600 °C |
| Power transmission cable | Electrical conductivity | Pure Cu (OFHC) or Al 1350 | $\sigma$ > 100% IACS |
| Cryogenic storage tank | Toughness at -196 °C | 304L austenitic SS | Stable FCC at low $T$, no ductile-to-brittle transition |
| Cookware | Thermal conduction + food-safe | 304 SS (18Cr-8Ni) | Non-reactive, easy to clean |
| Rocket nozzle throat | Ablation resistance | Tungsten or C-C composite | $T_m$ = 3422 °C, the highest of all metals |
| Bicycle frame | Fatigue + lightweight | Al 6061-T6 or Ti-3Al-2.5V | Optimized strength-to-weight |
| Steam turbine blade | High-T creep resistance | 12% Cr martensitic steel | $\sigma_y$ > 700 MPa at 600 °C |

### 3.6 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Determination of Phase Fractions in a Steel

A steel contains **0.8 wt% C** at room temperature (the eutectoid composition). The phases present are α-ferrite (BCC) and Fe₃C (cementite, orthorhombic).

At eutectoid temperature (727 °C), the austenite (γ-FCC) decomposes:
- α-ferrite has C solubility = 0.022 wt%
- Cementite has fixed C content = 6.70 wt%

The **Lever Rule** gives mass fractions:

$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\alpha}} = \frac{6.70 - 0.80}{6.70 - 0.022} = \frac{5.90}{6.678} = 0.883
$$

$$
W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\alpha}} = \frac{0.80 - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022} = \frac{0.778}{6.678} = 0.117
$$

**Verification:** $W_{\alpha} + W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 0.883 + 0.117 = 1.000$ ✓

**Interpretation:** At the eutectoid point, the steel is **88.3% ferrite and 11.7% cementite** by mass. This is the basis of the well-known **Pearlite** microstructure (alternating lamellae of ferrite and cementite in 88.3:11.7 ratio).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Metal Alloys

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["METAL ALLOYS"] --> node2["FERROUS ALLOYS"]
    node1 --> node3["NON-FERROUS ALLOYS"]

    node2 --> node4["STEELS C less than 2.14 percent"]
    node2 --> node5["CAST IRONS C greater than 2.14 percent"]

    node4 --> node6["PLAIN CARBON STEEL"]
    node4 --> node7["ALLOY STEEL"]
    node4 --> node8["STAINLESS STEEL"]

    node6 --> node9["LOW CARBON"]
    node6 --> node10["MEDIUM CARBON"]
    node6 --> node11["HIGH CARBON"]

    node8 --> node12["AUSTENITIC 300 series"]
    node8 --> node13["FERRITIC 400 series"]
    node8 --> node14["MARTENSITIC"]
    node8 --> node15["DUPLEX"]

    node5 --> node16["WHITE CI"]
    node5 --> node17["GRAY CI"]
```
